content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

November 1, 1991


Dana W. Phillips
General Counsel
Sacramento Housing and
Redevelopment Agency
P.O. Box 1834
Sacramento, CA 95812-1834

Re: Your Request for Informal Asistance
Our File No. I-91-466

Dear Mr. Phillips:

This is in response to your letter requesting assistance on
behalf of Sacramento City Councilmember Heather Fargo concerning
her duties under the conflict-of-interest provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the "Act"). You are seeking additional
advice with respect to the proposed North Sacramento Redevelopment
Area. (See, Fargo Advice Letter, No. I-91-089.) You have
requested informal assistance.²

Please note that our advice is limited to the Political
Reform Act. The Commission does not advise on other areas of the
law pertaining to redevelopment. In addition, this letter is
based on the facts presented to us. The Commission does not act
as the finder of fact in rendering advice. (In re Ogelsby (1975)
1 FPPC Ops. 71.)

QUESTIONS

1. May Councilmember Fargo participate in the adoption of a
proposed redevelopment plan for the North Sacramento Redevelopment
Area, including the selection of boundaries, approval of a project

_______________
1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section
83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)


428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916)322-5660

Our File No. I-91-466
Page 2


area committee, the designation of land uses within the area, and
the approval of the preliminary plan for the project?

     2.  May Councilmember Fargo participate in future decisions
concerning implementation of the various projects within the North
Sacramento Redevelopment Area?

     3.  Will the decisions on the North Sacramento Redevelopment
Area affect the public generally in the same manner as they will
affect the interests of the councilmember?

     4.  May Councilmember Fargo participate in Sacramento City
Council decisions on redistricting?

                              CONCLUSIONS

     1.  Councilmember Fargo may not participate in the adoption
of a proposed redevelopment plan for the North Sacramento
Redevelopment Area, the selection of boundaries, approval of a
project area committee, the designation of land uses within the
area, or the approval of the preliminary plan for the project if
she or her spouse owns property within the redevelopment area.

     2.  Councilmember Fargo may participate in future decisions
concerning implementation of the various projects within the North
Sacramento Redevelopment Area provided the future decisions are
not interrelated to the decisions for which the councilmember was
disqualified, and the councilmember has no other conflicts of
interest with respect to the decision.

     3.  The "public generally" exception will not permit
Councilmember Fargo to participate unless the decisions will
affect a significant segment of Sacramento in the same manner as
the decisions will affect the interests of the councilmember.
Assuming that all the landowners and residents in the North
Sacramento Redevelopment Area will be affected in a substantially
similar manner by the decisions, the percentage of the population
of Sacramento in the North Sacramento Redevelopment Area is too
small to be considered a significant segment of the public
generally.

     4.  Decisions concerning redistricting in Sacramento will not
foreseeably affect the councilmember's interests.  Consequently,
Councilmember Fargo is not precluded from participating in the
redistricting decisions due to the economic interests discussed in
this letter.

                              FACTS

     Councilmember Fargo is the Sacramento City Councilmember for
District One of the city.  The Sacramento City Council will be
considering the adoption of a proposed redevelopment plan for the
North Sacramento Redevelopment Area.  The decisions will include
the selection of boundaries, approval of a project area committee,

Our File No. I-91-466
Page 3


the designation of land uses within the area, and the approval of
the preliminary plan for the project. The City is also
considering a redistricting plan which would include a portion of
the proposed redevelopment area in Councilmember Fargo's district.

The proposed boundaries of the North Sacramento project area
include approximately 1,200 acres out of approximately 5,000 acres
which were in the redevelopment survey area. The proposed
boundaries were selected because they encompass the most blighted
areas in the survey area. The proposed project area consists of
36 percent industrial-zoned property and 28 percent property zoned
for commercial and office uses. You estimated that approximately
1,700 different persons own property within the proposed project
area. Some of property owners in the area own more than a single
parcel.

Councilmember Fargo's spouse is acquiring real property for
his business which is located in the redevelopment survey area and
proposed project area. The property is currently zoned for
industrial uses and is one-half acre in size. The councilmember's
spouse plans to rehabilitate the property for his business
consistent with current zoning.

ANALYSIS

Economic Interests

As discussed in the Fargo Advice Letter, Section 87100 of the
Act prohibits any public official from making, participating in
making, or otherwise using his or her official position to
influence a governmental decision in which the official has a
financial interest. Councilmember Fargo is a "public official" as
defined in the Act. (Section 82048.)

Section 87103 specifies that an official has a financial
interest within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial
effect, distinguishable from the effect on the public generally,
on the official or a member of his or her immediate family or on:

(a) Any business entity in which the public
official has a direct or indirect investment worth
one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more.

(b) Any real property in which the public
official has a direct or indirect interest worth
one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more.

* * *

For purposes of this section, indirect
investment or interest means any investment or
interest owned by the spouse or dependent child of
a public official, by an agent on behalf of a

Our File No. I-91-466
Page 4


public official, or by a business entity or trust
in which the official, the official's agents,
spouse, and dependent children own directly,
indirectly, or beneficially a 10-percent interest
or greater.

Section 87103(a) and (b),
emphasis added.

You stated that Councilmember Fargo's spouse is acquiring
real property within the proposed redevelopment area for his
business. The Act provides:

"Interest in real property" includes any
leasehold, beneficial or ownership interest or an
option to acquire such an interest in real property
located in the jurisdiction owned directly,
indirectly or beneficially by the public official,
or other filer, or his or her immediate family if
the fair market value of the interest is one
thousand dollars ($1,000) or more. Interests in
real property of an individual includes a pro rata
share of interests in real property of any business
entity or trust in which the individual or
immediate family owns, directly, indirectly or
beneficially, a 10-percent interest or greater.

Section 82033.

"Investment" means any financial interest in
securities issued by a business entity, including
but not limited to common stock, preferred stock,
rights, warrants, options, debt instruments and any
partnership or other ownership interest owned
directly, indirectly or beneficially by the public
official, or other filer, or his or her immediate
family, if the business entity or any parent,
subsidiary or otherwise related business entity has
an interest in real property in the jurisdiction,
or does business or plans to do business in the
jurisdiction, or has done business within the
jurisdiction at any time during the two years prior
to the time any statement or other action is
required under this title. No asset shall be
deemed an investment unless its fair market value
equals or exceeds one thousand dollars ($1,000).
The term "investment" does not include a time or
demand deposit in a financial institution, shares
in a credit union, any insurance policy, interest
in a diversified mutual fund registered with the
Securities and Exchange Commission under the
Investment Company Act of 1940 or a common trust
fund which is created pursuant to Section 1564 of

Our File No. I-91-466
Page 5


the Financial Code, or any bond or other debt
instrument issued by any government or government
agency.  Investments of an individual includes a
pro rata share of investments of any business
entity, mutual fund, or trust in which the
individual or immediate family owns, directly,
indirectly or beneficially, a 10-percent interest
or greater.

Section 82034.

Thus, the councilmember has an indirect ownership interest in
her spouse's business.  If the councilmember's spouse acquires the
property in question, even in the name of his business, she will
be deemed to own a pro-rata share of the real property.  (Section
82033.)  Thus, for example, if the councilmember's spouse is the
sole owner of his business, he is deemed to wholly own the
property in question.  If he is a 50 percent partner, he is deemed
to own 50 percent of the real property.  Councilmember Pargo will
have an indirect economic interest in any property or business
interest of her spouse.  (Section 87103.)  Consequently, if her
indirect interest in the business and the real property is worth
more than $1,000, she is required to disqualify herself from
making or participating in any decision which would have a
foreseeable and material financial effect on the economic
interests.

Foreseeability

Whether the financial effects of a decision are reasonably
foreseeable at the time a governmental decision is made depends on
the facts of each particular case.  An effect is considered
reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial likelihood that
it will occur.  Certainty is not required.  However, if an effect
is only a mere possibility, it is not reasonably foreseeable.
(Downey Cares v. Downey Community Development Commission (1987)
196 Cal.App.3d 938; In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.)

As we discussed in the Fargo Advice Letter, the anticipated
result of any redevelopment plan is to increase the property
values and improve the business climate within the project area.
The very nature of redevelopment projects has led the Commission
to find that it is reasonably foreseeable that there will be a
financial effect on real property values and business interests
located within or near project areas.  (In re Ogelsby (1975) 1
FPPC Ops. 71; Armento Advice Letter, No. A-90-499.)  If the
councilmember has an interest in real property and a business
located in the redevelopment area, we presume that a financial
effect on these interests is foreseeable.

Our File No. I-91-466
Page 6


          However, decisions concerning the modification of city
     council districts will not foreseeably affect the councilmember's
     economic interests.  Consequently, Councilmember Fargo is not
     precluded from participating in the redistricting decisions due to
     the economic interests discussed in this letter.3

     Materiality

          However, the councilmember must only disqualify herself if
     the foreseeable effect on her economic interest is also material.
     The Commission has adopted a series of regulations which provide
     guidance concerning whether the foreseeable financial effects of a
     decision are material.  (Regulation 18702.)  These regulations
     apply different standards depending on whether the decision will
     directly or indirectly affect the official's economic interest.

          As we described in the Fargo Advice Letter, the materiality
     of the financial effect depends on whether the economic interest
     is directly involved in the decision or indirectly involved.
     (Regulation 18702.)  If the decision directly affects the
     official's real property interests, disqualification is required
     unless the decision will have no financial effect on the real
     property interest.  (Regulation 18702.1.)  An official's real
     property interest is directly involved in a redevelopment decision
     if:

               The decision is to designate the survey area,
          to select the project area, to adopt the
          preliminary plan, to form a project area committee,
          to certify the environmental document, to adopt the
          redevelopment plan, to add territory to the
          redevelopment area, or to rescind or amend any of
          the above decisions; and real property in which the
          official has an interest, or any part of it is
          located within the boundaries (or the proposed
          boundaries) of the redevelopment area.

               Regulation 18702.1(a)(3)(D).

          Thus, if the decision before the agency is to designate the
     survey area, to select the project area, to adopt the preliminary
     plan, to form a project area committee, to certify the
     environmental document, to adopt the redevelopment plan, to add
     territory to the redevelopment area, or to rescind or amend any of


     3    This assumes, of course, that the redistricting is not in any
     way linked or a necessary prerequisite to decisions for which the
     councilmember is disqualified.  Please see the discussion of
     segmentation below.

Our File No. I-91-466
Page 7


the above decisions, the councilmember must disqualify herself
from the decision.⁴

     However, other decisions not set forth in Regulation
18702.1(a)(3)(D) must be analyzed independently to determine if
there will be a foreseeable material financial effect on an
official's economic interests, provided the decisions are not too
interrelated to the decision for which the councilmember is
disqualified.⁵ For example, if the resolution of a later
redevelopment decision will effectively reverse the result of the
decision for which the councilmember had a conflict of interest,
the decisions are interrelated and the councilmember must
disqualify herself as to both decisions. The councilmember is
also required to disqualify itself as to all decisions which may
not be legally separated from any decision for which she has a
conflict of interest. This would be the case, for example, if
different results in the two decisions could result in a legal
challenge which would successfully compel the supervisors to
arrive at a consistent result. (Nord Advice Letter, No. A-82-038;
See also, Miller Advice Letter, No. A-82-119.)

     Decisions implementing a redevelopment plan are generally
separable from decisions to establish the redevelopment area. For
example, in our letter to Lester J. Marston (Marston Advice Letter
No. A-89-120) we discussed a corporation's application to the City
of Willits for a use permit to operate a cogeneration power plant
facility within the city's redevelopment zone. The city council
was to consider the use permit and a series of related decisions
concerning possible mitigation measures proposed in the
environmental impact report. Four members of the city council
owned property within the redevelopment zone. However, since the
decision concerned the environmental impact report for a specific
project rather than the redevelopment plan as a whole, Regulation
18702.1(a)(3)(D) was not applicable. (See, Marston Advice Letter,
supra, and Rosenthal Advice Letter, No. I-89-406.)

___________________________
     ⁴ Please note that the decision may also have a foreseeable and
material financial effect with respect to the business of the
councilmember's spouse. If a determination is reached that the
property in question will not be materially affected or that the
business will not purchase the property, the financial effects on
the business must still be analyzed to determine whether the
councilmember may participate. Where the business does not own
property within the redevelopment area, Regulation 18702.2 should
be consulted to determine materiality. We have enclosed a copy
for your information.

     ⁵ Moreover, even the decisions listed in Regulation 18702.1 may
need to be reanalyzed as the redevelopment plan progresses. For
example, if the decision on the boundaries of the redevelopment
area ultimately excluded the property of the councilmember's
spouse, the question would need to be reanalyzed under Regulation
18702.3. We have enclosed this regulation for your information.

Our File No. I-91-466
Page 8


Thus, where decisions concern discrete projects in the
redevelopment area, the decisions must be analyzed independently
to determine if the councilmember may still be required to
disqualify herself from the decisions.

According to Regulation 18702.1(a)(3), the financial effect
of a decision on an official's real property is material and
disqualification required if the decision involves:

(1) The zoning or rezoning, annexation or deannexation,
sale, purchase, or lease, or inclusion in or exclusion from any
city, county, district or other local governmental subdivision, of
the official's real property (Regulation 18702.1(a)(3)(A));

(2) The decision involves the issuance, denial or revocation
of a license, permit or other land use entitlement authorizing a
specific use or uses of the official's real property (Regulation
18702.1(a)(3)(B)); or

(3) The decision involves the imposition, repeal or
modification of any taxes or fees assessed or imposed on the
official's real property (Regulation 18702.1(a)(3)(C));

Moreover, where a decision does not fall into one of the
categories set forth above, the councilmember's real property may
still be indirectly materially affected and therefore require
disqualification. The indirect effect of a decision on an
official's real property interest is material if:

(1) The real property in which the official
has an interest, or any part of that real property,
is located within a 300 foot radius of the
boundaries (or the proposed boundaries) of the
property which is the subject of the decision,
unless the decision will have no financial effect
upon the official's real property interest.

(2) The decision involves construction of, or
improvements to, streets, water, sewer, storm
drainage or similar facilities, and the real
property in which the official has an interest will
receive new or substantially improved services.

(3) The real property in which the official
has an interest is located outside a radius of 300
feet and any part of the real property is located
within a radius of 2,500 feet of the boundaries (or
the proposed boundaries) of the property which is

Our File No. I-91-466
Page 9


the subject of the decision and the decision will
have a reasonably foreseeable financial effect of:

     (A)  Ten thousand dollars ($10,000) or
more on the fair market value of the real
property in which the official has an
interest; or

     (B)  Will affect the rental value of the
property by $1,000 or more per 12 month
period.

          Regulation 18702.3(a).

If the foreseeable financial effect of any governmental
decision will meet the thresholds set forth in the applicable
provision of Regulation 18702.3, the councilmember may not
participate in the decision.

     Where other decisions concerning the redevelopment project
are not interrelated to the decisions from which the councilmember
was disqualified, the following procedure should be followed to
permit Councilmember Fargo to participate:

     (1)  The decisions from which the councilmember has a
disqualifying financial interest should be segregated from the
other decisions.

     (2)  The decisions from which the councilmember is
disqualified should be considered first, and a final decision
reached by the city council without Councilmember Fargo
participating in any way.

     (3)  Once a decision has been made on the portions of the
project for which the councilmember has a disqualifying interest,
the councilmember may participate in the deliberations regarding
other portions of the proposed project, so long as those
deliberations do not result in a reopening or in any way affect
the decisions from which the councilmember was disqualified.
(Huffaker Advice Letter, No. A-86-343.)

The "Public Generally" Exception

     Public officials with real property interests that will be
financially affected by the decision may participate if the effect
on their property is not distinguishable from the effect on the
publicly.6  For the "public generally" exception to apply,

_______________
6    The "public" consists of the entire jurisdiction of the agency
in question.  (In re Owen (1976) 2 FPPC Ops. 77.)  This is so
because all the residents of the jurisdiction are constituents of
the official.  (In re Leman (1985) 9 FPPC Ops. 1.)

Our File No. I-91-466
Page 10


a decision must affect the official's interests in substantially
the same manner as it would affect either (1) the entire
population of the jurisdiction, or (2) a significant segment of
the population. (Regulation 18703.)

Clearly the establishment of a redevelopment area will not
affect all persons in the jurisdiction in substantially the same
manner. Those persons owning property in the area will be more
directly and substantially affected than those owning property in
other parts of Sacramento. Consequently, for the "public interest
generally" exception to apply to this situation, the redevelopment
decisions would have to affect the entire population in the
decisions would have to affect the entire population in the
redevelopment area in substantially the same manner, and the
population in the redevelopment area would need to constitute a
significant segment of the population of Sacramento.

You stated that approximately 1,700 different persons owned
property within the redevelopment area. The population in the
City of Sacramento is 346,586 persons. Thus, the segment owning
property in the redevelopment area is less than one-half of one
percent of the population of the city. This would not constitute
a significant segment of Sacramento. (Scher Advice Letter, No. 88-
479.)

If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at (916) 322-5901.7

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel

By: John W. Wallace
Counsel, Legal Division

SH:JWW:aa


7 Copies of Commission regulations and opinions may be obtained
from the county law library. Copies of advice letters referenced
in this letter may be obtained from the Commission at a cost of
10¢ per page.

embedding.first_500_words: California Fair Political Practices Commission November 1, 1991 Dana W. Phillips General Counsel Sacramento Housing and Redevelopment Agency P.O. Box 1834 Sacramento, CA 95812-1834 Re: Your Request for Informal Asistance Our File No. I-91-466 Dear Mr. Phillips: This is in response to your letter requesting assistance on behalf of Sacramento City Councilmember Heather Fargo concerning her duties under the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act"). You are seeking additional advice with respect to the proposed North Sacramento Redevelopment Area. (See, Fargo Advice Letter, No. I-91-089.) You have requested informal assistance.² Please note that our advice is limited to the Political Reform Act. The Commission does not advise on other areas of the law pertaining to redevelopment. In addition, this letter is based on the facts presented to us. The Commission does not act as the finder of fact in rendering advice. (In re Ogelsby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) QUESTIONS 1. May Councilmember Fargo participate in the adoption of a proposed redevelopment plan for the North Sacramento Redevelopment Area, including the selection of boundaries, approval of a project _______________ 1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section 18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations. 2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).) 428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916)322-5660 Our File No. I-91-466 Page 2 area committee, the designation of land uses within the area, and the approval of the preliminary plan for the project? 2. May Councilmember Fargo participate in future decisions concerning implementation of the various projects within the North Sacramento Redevelopment Area? 3. Will the decisions on the North Sacramento Redevelopment Area affect the public generally in the same manner as they will affect the interests of the councilmember? 4. May Councilmember Fargo participate in Sacramento City Council decisions on redistricting? CONCLUSIONS 1. Councilmember Fargo may not participate in the adoption of a proposed redevelopment plan for the North Sacramento Redevelopment Area, the selection of boundaries, approval of a project area committee, the designation of land uses within the area, or the approval of the preliminary plan for the project if she or her spouse owns property within the redevelopment area. 2. Councilmember Fargo may participate in future decisions concerning implementation of the various projects within the North Sacramento Redevelopment Area provided the future decisions are not interrelated to the decisions for which the councilmember was disqualified, and the councilmember has no other conflicts of interest with respect to the decision. 3. The "public generally" exception will not permit Councilmember Fargo to participate unless the decisions will affect a significant segment of Sacramento in the same manner as the decisions will affect the interests of the councilmember.
